document.docAuthor: Kataev, Boris M., Hongbin, Liang & Wrase, David W.
document.masterDocTitle: New data on carabid beetles of Trichotichnus s. str. (Coleoptera: Carabidae) of Yunnan (China) and adjacent areas, with description of six new species and two new subspecies

mods:titleInfo.mods:title: New data on carabid beetles of Trichotichnus s. str. (Coleoptera: Carabidae) of Yunnan (China) and adjacent areas, with description of six new species and two new subspecies

mods:name.mods:affiliation: Zoological Institute, Russian Academy of Sciences, Universitetskaya nab. 1, St. Petersburg 199034, Russia.

Key laboratory of Zoological Systematics and Evolution, Institute of Zoology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100101, China. lianghb @ ioz. ac. cn; https: // orcid. org / 0000 - 0002 - 9668 - 1167

subSubSection.paragraph:
Type material examined. Holotype of
T. cyrtops
: male, “Dol. r. Tunk-go-kho, 25.VII.93, Potanin” [in Cyrillic], “
A. cyrtops
m., typ., Tschitscherin det.”, “
Trichotichnus (s. str.) cyrtops Tschitsch.
74 Lafer det.” ( ZIN ).

Additional material examined.

Thailand .
CHIANG MAI : 1 female , Angkhang , 19°54.157‘N 99°02.486‘E ,
19–20.X.2007
, U. Scheidt leg. ( NME )
.
Re-description ( 5 males and 5 females measured). Body length 7.7–9.2 mm . Habitus as in Fig. 35 .

caption.paragraph:
FIGURES 35–37.
Trichotichnus spp.
, habitus. 35,
T. cyrtops ( Tschitschérine, 1906 )
(Yunnan); 36,
T. doiinthanonensis Ito, 1997
(Dulongjiang); 37,
T. fedorenkoi
sp. n.
(Tam Dao). Scale bar = 1.0 mm.

subSubSection.paragraph: Body dorsally dark brown to almost black, shiny, slightly iridescent on elytra; ventral side blackish brown; mandibles basally, labrum, apical margin of clypeus, narrow margins of pronotum reddish or yellowish brown; palpi, antennae and legs brownish yellow to light brown; femora not darker than tibiae.

Head with large and convex eyes (HWmax/PWmax 0.67–0.71, HWmin/PWmax 0.52–0.56, HWmax/HWmin 1.24–1.33). Genae very narrow, narrower than or about as wide as antennomere 2 basally. Fronto-ocular furrows fine, deepened at clypeus, reaching supraorbital furrows. Supraorbital setigerous pores small, situated at level of posterior margin of eye and removed from supraorbital furrows by distance approximately equal to width of antennomere 1. Labrum slightly concave anteriorly. Left mandible sharply bent in the apical quarter, blunted or subtruncate at tip. Mentum and submentum not fused. Ligular sclerite markedly widened apically. Paraglossae short and narrow, not projecting anteriorly beyond ligular sclerite. Dorsal microsculpture highly obliterated, consisting of indistinct, more or less isodiametric meshes. Antennae slender, surpassing pronotal basal edge by two apical antennomeres, with antennomeres 4–8 about 2.0–2.5 times as long as wide. Basal antennomere very slightly longer than antennomere 3.

Pronotum relatively small, transverse (PWmax/PL 1.46, 1.51–1.59), widest slightly before the middle (PWmax/ PWmin 1.15–1.22), more strongly narrowed apically than basally, with base slightly longer than apex and with sides in most specimens rounded along entire length, more widely in basal half than in apical half; occasionally, almost straight basally. Apical margin moderately emarginate, basal margin almost straight or slightly convex medially and scarcely oblique laterally, both completely bordered. Lateral bead complete, narrow. Basal angle obtusangular, sharp at apex, without denticle at tip. Disc moderately convex; lateral depressions very narrow in apical third, moderately widened from lateral setae posteriorly, either reaching basal margin or fused basally with basal foveae forming on each side wide laterobasal depression; punctation fine and dense, restricted mainly to lateral and laterobasal depressions, more sparse punctures also present in narrow medio-basal area and occasionally at apical margin. Microsculpture very fine, consisting of obliterated transverse meshes.

Elytra convex, elongate (EL/EW 1.48–1.56, EL/PL 2.90–3.14, EW/PWmax 1.26–1.32), widest in the apical third, with sides slightly concave or almost straightly diverging before the middle; humeri rounded, with a tiny denticle recognizable in caudal view; preapical sinuation moderately deep ( Fig. 38 ); sutural angle acutangular, blunted at tip. Internal humeral angle very obtuse. Striae slightly impressed along entire length. Intervals weakly convex on disc and moderately convex apically. Parascutellar (abbreviate) striole long, with a setigerous pore at base. Interval 3 with a small discal setigerous pore adjacent to stria 2 in the apical third. Marginal umbilicate series more or less continuous or narrowly interrupted at the middle. Microsculpture consisting of highly obliterated, somewhat indistinct transverse lines.

caption.paragraph:
FIGURES 38–39.
Trichotichnus spp.
, elytral apex. 38,
T. cyrtops ( Tschitschérine, 1906 )
; 39,
T. schmidti
sp. n.
Scale bar = 0.5 mm.

FIGURES 40–45.
Trichotichnus spp.
: 40–43,
T. cyrtops ( Tschitschérine, 1906 )
(holotype and female from Sichuan); 44, 45,
T. emarginatus Andrewes, 1930
(Nepal). 40, 41, 44 & 45, median lobe of aedeagus; 42, gonocoxite; 43, laterotergite, gonosubcoxite, and gonocoxite. Lateral (40, 42 & 44), dorsal (41 & 45) and ventral (43) views. Scale bars: A = 0.5 mm (42 & 43), B = 1.0 mm (40, 41, 44 & 45).

FIGURE 46.
Trichotichnus cyrtops ( Tschitschérine, 1906 )
, distribution (localities from the Vietnamese Province of Lam Dong are not indicated).

subSubSection.paragraph: Wings fully developed.
Prosternum with very fine short setae. Metepisternum much longer than wide, strongly narrowed posteriorly.

Legs relatively short. Metacoxa without additional setae. Metafemur ventrally with two setae at posterior margin and without setae at anterior margin. Protibia on dorsal side without longitudinal sulcus, on outer margin preapically with one stouter and two very thin spines in male and with three comparatively stout, similar sized spines in female. Tarsi glabrous dorsally, pro- and mesotarsomeres 1–4 in both sexes with comparatively sparse moderately long ventro-lateral setae. Metatarsus slender, slightly shorter than HWmax, with tarsomeres narrow, weakly widened posteriorly and with tarsomere 1 almost as long as tarsomeres 2 and 3 combined. In male, pro- and mesotarsomeres 2–4 dilated and with adhesive vestiture ventrally (mesotarsomere 1 narrow, only with a pair of adhesive scales apically); protarsomeres 2 and 3 about as long as wide; mesotarsomeres 2 and 3 slightly longer than wide; in both sexes mesotarsomere 1 almost as long as mesotarsomeres 2 and 3 combined.

Comparison. This species is recognized by having the following combination of distinctive characters: appendages brownish yellow to light brown, pronotum with rounded sides and distinct obtuse basal angles without denticle at tip, elytra with rather deep preapical sinuation, protibia not sulcate on dorsal side, last visible abdominal sternite of male with one pair of marginal setae and median lobe of aedeagus almost straight in lateral view, with long and wide terminal lamella. In these characters and other features,
T. cyrtops
is very similar to
T. emarginatus Andrewes, 1930
which differs in slightly smaller (length 6.6–8.2 mm ) and flatter body and in the median lobe of the aedeagus ( Figs. 44 & 45 ) more strongly curved apically in lateral view and more strongly sinuate in dorsal view ( Kataev & Schmidt 2018 ). These two species are undoubtedly closely related and represent a sister pair; in the pe- culiar shape of the aedeagus, they are markedly separated from other species of the genus.

Distribution ( Fig. 46 ). Widely distributed in South West China ( Sichuan and Yunnan ) and Indochina ( Laos , Vietnam and Thailand ) at altitudes of about 900–3000 m . It is replaced in the Himalayan region by the allopatric
T. emarginatus
which occurs there almost throughout from northeasternmost India through Nepal to northwestenmost India ( Kataev & Schmidt 2018 ).

Remarks.
Trichotichnus cyrtops
was described on the basis of one male from Sichuan ( type locality: “Tunkgo-kho Valley”; = Dadu He Valley, near Danba/Rongzhag), whereas
T. baehri
from the series collected in Thailand ( type locality: Doi Inthanon, Chiang Mai Province ). In the original description ( Ito 1997 ),
T. baehri
was compared only with
T. noctuabundus Habu, 1954
and
T. ishiharai Ito, 1994
, but since its characteristics, including of male genitalia, completely match those of
T. cyrtops
, it was treated as conspecific with the latter species ( Kataev & Wrase 2017a: 18 ).
